---
title: "W14L1: Time Series"
subtitle: "POLS 642 Intermediate Analysis of Political Data"
author: "Dr. Ches Thurber"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
execute: 
  echo: true
editor: visual
---

## Plan for Today

1\) "Bell Ringer"

2\) Fixed Effects Review

3\) TSCS Data

4\) BTSCS Data

## Packages

```{r}
#install.packages("ggdag")
library(gapminder)
library(ggdag)
library(ggplot2)
library(modelsummary)
library(dplyr)
#install.packages("flextable")
library(flextable)
#install.packages("fixest")
library(fixest)
#install.packages("stevemisc")
library(stevemisc)

```

## Bell-Ringer

Let's say I wanted to get more precise results from my Gapminder analysis of GDP and Life Expectancy. A friend says "Why don't you move from country-year to country-month units of analysis? Then you will have 12x the sample size, reducing your standard errors!"

Is there anything wrong with this?

## Types of Datasets

1\) Cross-sectional data: single observations from each unit (survey responses from a single wave)

2\) Time series data: multiple observations over time from a single unit (daily stock prices)

3\) Panel data: multiple observations over time from multiple unites (aka Time Series Cross-Sectional, TSCS)

## Strengths and Weaknesses

-   allows you to get more observations from your data (maybe)

-   track within group variation

-   But data from within groups will be highly correlated

-   FEs was one approach to dealing with this

# Fixed Effects

## What Do We Do?The Logic of Fixed Effects

-   If you have grouped data...

-   Those groups are likely correlated with confounding variables

-   By controlling for the group (as a categorical variable), you control for all potentially confounding variables that are correlated with the group

## Different Intercepts, Same Slope

![](https://theeffectbook.net/the-effect_files/figure-html/fixedeffects-interceptdiff-1.png)

## Fixed Effects as Diffs from Group Mean

```{r, echo = F}
gm <- gapminder
Model0 <- lm(lifeExp~log(gdpPercap), data = gapminder)
```

```{r}
gm <- gm %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(lifeExp_within = lifeExp-mean(lifeExp),
         log_gdpPercap_groupmean = mean(log(gdpPercap)),
         log_gdpPercap_within = log(gdpPercap) - mean(log(gdpPercap)))

Model1 <- lm(lifeExp_within ~ log_gdpPercap_within, data = gm)
modelsummary(list("Pooled" = Model0, "Absorbed FE" = Model1), stars= T, gof_map = c("nobs","r.squared"), coef_map = c("log(gdpPercap)" = "GDP per capita (ln)", "log_gdpPercap_within"= "GDP per capita (ln)"), vcov = ~country, output = "flextable"
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

# TSCS Data

## Lagged DV (AR: Autoregression)

-   you might alternatively choose to include a measure of the DV in the prior year

```{r}
gm <- gm %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(lag_lifeExp = lag(lifeExp))

Model_AR <- lm(lifeExp ~ log(gdpPercap) + lag_lifeExp, data = gm)
modelsummary(list("Pooled" = Model0, "Absorbed FE" = Model1, "Lagged DV" = Model_AR), stars= T, gof_map = c("nobs","r.squared"), coef_map = c("log(gdpPercap)" = "GDP per capita (ln)", "log_gdpPercap_within"= "GDP per capita (ln)", "lag_lifeExp" = "Life Exp. (t-1)"), vcov = ~country, output = "flextable"
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

## Delta-Delta Models

```{r}
gm <- gm %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(delta_lifeExp = lifeExp - lag_lifeExp,
         delta_log_gdpPercap = log(gdpPercap) - lag(log(gdpPercap)))

Model_Delta <- lm(delta_lifeExp ~ delta_log_gdpPercap, data = gm)
modelsummary(list("Pooled" = Model0, "Absorbed FE" = Model1, "Lagged DV" = Model_AR, "Deltas" = Model_Delta), stars= T, gof_map = c("nobs","r.squared"), coef_map = c("log(gdpPercap)" = "GDP per capita (ln)", "log_gdpPercap_within"= "GDP per capita (ln)", "delta_log_gdpPercap" = "Change in GDP", "lag_lifeExp" = "Life Exp. (t-1)"), vcov = ~country, output = "flextable"
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

## More Advanced Choices

-   Moving Average: not just last year's DV, but the average over a preceding period of time

-   ARMA: lagged DV and moving average terms

-   ARIMA: similar to deltas model described above

-   multiple levels of lags and moving average processes...

## This is hard!

-   so many choices to make: fixed effects? ARMA? ARIMA? ARIMAX? SARIMAX?

-   best practice is to find an article modeling your DV or something similar, and follow their approach

# BTSCS Data

## When the DV is Binary

-   rather than thinking about a lagged DV, we might think about how many time increments have passed since the last occurrence of the DV

-   We will call these "spells" or "time spells"

-   it used to be that we would compute complex functions called "splines"

-   now we will just throw time spells into our model as controls, with squared and cubic polynomial terms

## Let's Study Civil Wars

```{r}
FL <- read.csv("FearonLaitin.csv")
head(FL)
```

## Create Time Spells

```{r}
FL <- ps_btscs(FL, war, year, country)
```

## Create Regressions With and Without

```{r}
FL_NoSpells <- glm(war~lgdpenl1, data = FL, family = "binomial")
FL_Spells <- glm(war~lgdpenl1 + spell + I(spell^2) + I(spell^3), data = FL, family = "binomial")
msummary(list(FL_NoSpells, FL_Spells), stars = T, vcov = ~country, output = "flextable"
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

## Conclusion

-   panel data offers lots of opportunities for examining more fine-grained variation

-   studying long-term changes in continuous variables over time is hard

-   with binary DVs, it's a lot easier

-   pay attention to and copy practices you see in the literature
